exercises.title: Define your first method
exercises.scenario: Define a method that prints a greeting.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to define a void method called `sayHi` that prints 'Hi there!'

blanks.hint: Keyword needed for class-level methods (so main can call it)
Return type for a method that returns nothing

hints: Method pattern: public static <return_type> name()
Use `static` (so it can be called from main)
Use `void` since the method doesn't return anything

exercises.title: Call a method multiple times
exercises.scenario: Use a method to print a message three times.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Triple` with:
1. A static void method called `printStar` that prints '*'
2. A main method that calls printStar() three times

Expected output:
*
*
*
hints: Define the method ABOVE or BELOW main — either works
Inside the method: System.out.println("*");
Call it three times in main

exercises.title: Trace method calls
exercises.scenario: Predict the order of output when methods call each other.
hints: main starts: prints A
Calls first(): prints B, then calls second()
second(): prints C, returns to first()
first() finishes, returns to main
main prints D

exercises.title: Extract repeated code into a method
exercises.scenario: Take repeated code and refactor it into a reusable method.
exercises.instructions:
The code below has duplicate println blocks. Refactor it: create a method `printSeparator` that prints `'---'`, then call it from main wherever the separator was used.

ORIGINAL OUTPUT (your refactored version should match):
---
Hello
---
World
---

Write the refactored version in a class called `Refactored`.
hints: Define printSeparator() as a static void method
Inside, just one println of '---'
In main, call printSeparator() three times (in place of the original println of '---')

exercises.title: Multiple methods, one main
exercises.scenario: Organize a simple report into separate methods.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `WeatherReport` with three methods called from main:
1. `printHeader()` — prints 'WEATHER REPORT'
2. `printTemp()` — prints 'Temperature: 28°C'
3. `printCondition()` — prints 'Condition: Sunny'

main() should call all three in order.

Expected output:
WEATHER REPORT
Temperature: 28°C
Condition: Sunny
hints: Three separate methods, each with one println
All three are public static void
main calls them in order: printHeader(), printTemp(), printCondition()
Order of method DEFINITIONS in file doesn't matter

exercises.title: Method calling another method
exercises.scenario: Build methods that call each other.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `Nested` with:
1. A method `printBorder()` that prints '======'
2. A method `printBoxed()` that calls printBorder, then prints 'TITLE', then calls printBorder again
3. main() that just calls printBoxed()

Expected output:
======
TITLE
======
hints: printBoxed() should call printBorder() twice and println once in between
Methods can freely call other methods in the same class
main only needs to call printBoxed() once

exercises.title: Fix the method definition errors
exercises.scenario: Your colleague wrote some method definitions with bugs. Fix them.
exercises.instructions:
This code has 3 bugs related to method syntax. Fix them so it compiles and prints:
Loading...
Done!

bugs.issue: Missing parentheses on showLoading. Without (), it's not a method call.
showLoading() is missing 'static' keyword. Can't call non-static from static main.
showDone() is missing return type. Every method needs a return type.

hints: Method calls need parentheses, even if no arguments: methodName()
All methods in this module need `static` (so main can call them)
Every method definition needs a return type — use `void` if returning nothing

exercises.title: Break down a long main
exercises.scenario: Take a monolithic main() and refactor it into multiple methods.
exercises.instructions:
The code below has everything in main. Refactor into a class `MenuApp` with these methods:
- `printTitle()` — prints 'MAIN MENU'
- `printOptions()` — prints '1. Start' / '2. Settings' / '3. Quit' (3 separate lines)
- `printPrompt()` — prints 'Choose an option:'

main should call them in order. Output should match exactly:
MAIN MENU
1. Start
2. Settings
3. Quit
Choose an option:

test_cases.expected_stdout:
MAIN MENU
1. Start
2. Settings
3. Quit
Choose an option:

hints: Each method has a specific job
printOptions() should contain ALL three option printlns
main becomes a high-level outline — just three method calls
This is the pattern: main = outline, methods = details

exercises.title: Naming methods well
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `BetterNamed` with the following methods (note the proper camelCase verb-based names):
- `printWelcomeMessage()` — prints 'Welcome to our app!'
- `displayCurrentDate()` — prints 'Date: 2026-05-18'
- `showUserStatus()` — prints 'Status: Active'

main calls them in order.

Expected output:
Welcome to our app!
Date: 2026-05-18
Status: Active
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your BetterNamed class below
// Use the EXACT method names specified — they demonstrate good naming

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Welcome to our app!
Date: 2026-05-18
Status: Active

hints: Method names start with a verb: print, display, show, get, set, calculate, etc.
Use camelCase (first letter lowercase, then capitalize each word)
Be specific — names should describe what the method does

exercises.title: Reuse a method multiple times
exercises.scenario: Demonstrate the DRY principle by reusing a method.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `BannerApp` with:
1. A method `printBanner()` that prints these 3 lines:
   `**********`
   `*  ALERT *`
   `**********`
2. main() should call printBanner() THREE times (showing it 3 times in a row)

Expected output:
**********
*  ALERT *
**********
**********
*  ALERT *
**********
**********
*  ALERT *
**********
hints: printBanner() has 3 println statements inside
main() calls printBanner() three times — written ONCE, used 3 times
Imagine writing this without methods — 9 println statements!

exercises.title: Multi-level method calls
exercises.scenario: Build a hierarchy of methods that call each other.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class `ReportGenerator` with this structure:
- `generateReport()` calls: `printHeader()`, `printBody()`, `printFooter()`
- `printHeader()` prints '=== REPORT ==='
- `printBody()` prints two lines: 'Item: 1' and 'Item: 2'
- `printFooter()` prints '--- END ---'
- main() just calls generateReport()

Expected output:
=== REPORT ===
Item: 1
Item: 2
--- END ---
hints: main() is super simple: just calls generateReport()
generateReport() coordinates three sub-methods
Each sub-method does its specific job
This 3-level structure (main → generateReport → details) is real-world code organization

exercises.title: Fix the missing call
exercises.scenario: Some methods are defined but never called.
exercises.instructions:
This code defines methods correctly but main() doesn't call them all. Fix main so it calls each defined method exactly once, producing the expected output.

Expected output:
START
Middle 1
Middle 2
END
hints: Defining a method isn't enough — you have to CALL it
Between 'START' and 'END' printlns, call middle1() and middle2()
Don't forget the parentheses on the calls

exercises.title: Refactor a long main
exercises.scenario: Take a 25-line main and refactor it into properly organized methods.
exercises.instructions:
Below is a long main(). Refactor it into a class `LoginScreen` with FOUR methods:
- `printAppLogo()` — prints the 3 logo lines
- `printLoginPrompt()` — prints the 'LOGIN' header line
- `printInputFields()` — prints the username and password prompts
- `printActions()` — prints the action buttons

main() should be 4 lines (just the method calls).

Expected output (must match exactly):
  ___ ___ 
 |    |   |
 |____|___|
========= LOGIN =========
Username: ________
Password: ________
[ Login ] [ Sign Up ] [ Forgot? ]
hints: main becomes a 4-line outline
Each method groups RELATED prints together
Logo: 3 lines together. Prompt: 1 line. Fields: 2 lines. Actions: 1 line.
Carefully preserve spacing in the original printlns

exercises.title: Refactor with shared helper
exercises.scenario: Find a duplicate pattern and extract it into a helper method.
exercises.instructions:
The output has THREE 'box headers' that all use the same separator pattern. Write a class `Dashboard` that:
1. Has a helper method `printSeparator()` that prints '++++++++++'
2. Has methods `showStats()`, `showAlerts()`, `showRecent()` — each prints its title using printSeparator above and below
3. main() calls showStats, showAlerts, showRecent in order

Expected output:
++++++++++
STATS
++++++++++
++++++++++
ALERTS
++++++++++
++++++++++
RECENT
++++++++++
exercises.starter_code:
// Write your Dashboard class below
// Hint: printSeparator() is called from 6 places (top+bottom of each section)
hints: printSeparator() is the helper — written once, called from many methods
Each section method (showStats, showAlerts, showRecent) follows same pattern: separator, title, separator
If you wanted to change the separator, you'd only change it in printSeparator() — DRY in action

exercises.title: Refactor profile card with methods
exercises.scenario: Take the Profile Card mini-project from Module 1 (Topic 1.2) and refactor it using methods.
exercises.instructions:
Build a class `ProfileCard` that organizes a developer profile display using methods.

Use these class-level static variables (declared at the top of the class):
```java
static String name = "Priya Sharma";
static String username = "priya_codes";
static String title = "Backend Developer";
static int yearsExperience = 3;
static int repos = 47;
static int followers = 1284;
```

Define these methods:
1. `printBorder()` — prints '+----------------------------------+'
2. `printNameSection()` — prints name and username (two lines):
   Name: Priya Sharma
   @priya_codes
3. `printProfessionalInfo()` — prints title and experience:
   Title: Backend Developer
   Experience: 3 years
4. `printStats()` — prints repos and followers:
   Repos: 47
   Followers: 1284
5. main() — orchestrates: printBorder, printNameSection, printBorder, printProfessionalInfo, printBorder, printStats, printBorder

Expected output:
+----------------------------------+
Name: Priya Sharma
@priya_codes
+----------------------------------+
Title: Backend Developer
Experience: 3 years
+----------------------------------+
Repos: 47
Followers: 1284
+----------------------------------+
hints: Declare 6 static variables at the class level (above main)
Methods can access class-level static variables directly — no parameters needed yet
printBorder is called 4 times in main — DRY in action
Each section method handles 2 lines of related info
When you reach Topic 2.2, we'll improve this by passing data through parameters instead of class variables
